questions.question: Which finding pattern best supports narcolepsy type 1 when other causes of sleepiness have been addressed?
options: Insomnia symptoms at least 3 nights per week for 3 months with normal MSLT results
EDS for at least 3 months plus cataplexy with MSL <=8 minutes and two or more SOREMPs
Snoring with an AHI greater than 5 and no REM-onset sleep periods
Nightmares with complete recall during the second half of the night
questions.rationale: Narcolepsy type 1 is supported by chronic excessive daytime sleepiness with cataplexy and supportive MSLT/PSG evidence, including short mean sleep latency and at least two SOREMPs.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: For narcolepsy, do not stop at “sleepy.” Look for the REM-intrusion clues and the testing pattern.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

questions.question: During a narcolepsy workup, the overnight PSG shows a SOREMP. How can that PSG finding affect the next day MSLT interpretation?
options: It invalidates the MSLT automatically
It proves obstructive sleep apnea is the cause of sleepiness
It may count as one of the two required SOREMPs when diagnostic criteria are otherwise met
It replaces the need to measure mean sleep latency
questions.rationale: A SOREMP on the preceding nocturnal PSG may count as one of the two required SOREMPs for narcolepsy diagnosis when the rest of the criteria are met.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Remember: the overnight PSG is not just a gatekeeper; sometimes it contributes a SOREMP.

questions.question: Which CSF hypocretin-1 result supports narcolepsy type 1?

A normal value with no cataplexy
Any value above the laboratory reference range
<=110 pg/mL or less than one-third of mean normal values
questions.rationale: Low CSF hypocretin-1, commonly <=110 pg/mL or less than one-third of normal values, supports narcolepsy type 1.

questions.question: What is overlap syndrome?
options: Coexistence of obstructive sleep apnea and COPD
Coexistence of nightmares and sleepwalking only
Presence of both E1 and E2 electrodes
A PSG with both lights off and lights on
questions.rationale: Overlap syndrome refers to the coexistence of obstructive sleep apnea and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

questions.question: Why can REM sleep be especially risky in some neuromuscular disorders?
options: REM sleep increases all accessory muscle tone to maximum
Accessory respiratory muscle tone is reduced, so weak diaphragmatic function can lead to marked hypoxemia or hypoventilation
REM sleep prevents hypoventilation
REM sleep eliminates the need for CO2 monitoring
questions.rationale: During REM, accessory muscle tone is reduced. Patients with impaired respiratory muscle strength may develop REM-related hypoxemia or hypoventilation.
questions.source: Clinician's Guide

It always increases to 100%
It is unrelated to aging
It can be measured only by ECG
questions.rationale: Sleep efficiency tends to decrease with age, with more awakenings and lighter sleep contributing to lower efficiency in older adults.

questions.question: Which sleep architecture pattern is commonly seen with aging?
options: More N3 in all older adults and no N1
More N1/N2, less N3, and fewer/slower spindles and K-complexes
Permanent REM-onset sleep every night
Loss of all wake after sleep onset
questions.rationale: With aging, N1 and N2 tend to increase, N3 tends to decrease, and sleep spindles/K-complexes may become less numerous and slower.

questions.question: Which feature helps distinguish nightmare disorder from sleep terrors?
options: Complete amnesia with N3 arousal only
Mandatory oxygen desaturation
Presence of at least four limb movements
Detailed dream recall after frightening dreams, usually from REM sleep
questions.rationale: Nightmare disorder involves frightening dreams with detailed recall, usually arising from REM sleep. Sleep terrors usually arise from NREM sleep and are often associated with amnesia.
questions.source: ICSD-3-TR

Eating during sleep with amnesia only

questions.question: During PAP titration, when may supplemental oxygen be considered according to the staged source layer?
options: When awake supine room-air SpO2 is <=88%, or SpO2 remains <=88% for at least 5 minutes after obstructive events are controlled
Whenever the patient snores once, regardless of SpO2
Before trying PAP in all patients
Only when actigraphy shows wakefulness
questions.rationale: Supplemental oxygen may be added during PAP titration when baseline awake supine room-air SpO2 is <=88%, or when SpO2 remains <=88% for at least 5 minutes after obstructive events are controlled.
questions.source: Clinical Atlas/PAP Titration Guideline
questions.coachBob: Oxygen can treat saturation, not obstruction. Fix obstruction first, then follow the oxygen rule.

questions.question: Which formula correctly expresses Ohm's Law?
options: V = I / R
I = V x R
R = V x I
V = I x R
questions.rationale: Ohm's Law is V = I x R. Rearranged, current equals voltage divided by resistance.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Voltage pushes, resistance resists, current flows.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

questions.question: If voltage remains constant and resistance increases, what happens to current?
options: Current decreases.
Current increases.
Current becomes frequency.
Current is unchanged because resistance does not affect it.
questions.rationale: Current equals voltage divided by resistance. With voltage constant, increasing resistance decreases current.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: High resistance is a traffic jam for electrons.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

questions.question: A circuit has 10 volts across 5 ohms of resistance. What is the current?
options: 5 amperes
2 amperes
15 amperes
50 amperes
questions.rationale: Using I = V/R, current = 10/5 = 2 amperes.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Write the formula before you touch the answer choices.

questions.question: What does Common Mode Rejection Ratio describe?

A differential amplifier's ability to cancel unwanted common signals such as 60 Hz interference

questions.question: Aliasing is most likely to occur when a signal is sampled at what rate?

More than 10 times its lowest frequency
Less than twice its highest frequency

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Severinghaus correction in transcutaneous CO2 monitoring?
options: To adjust measured PCO2 to 37°C and account for metabolic offset to estimate arterial PaCO2
To convert oximetry pulse rate into an apnea-hypopnea index
To remove ECG artifact from the chin EMG channel
To calculate total sleep time from sleep period time
questions.rationale: The Severinghaus correction adjusts measured PCO2 to 37°C and accounts for metabolic offset when estimating arterial PaCO2 from transcutaneous CO2.
questions.source: AAST TCM
questions.coachBob: TcCO2 is an estimate. The correction is part of making that estimate clinically meaningful.

questions.question: Which montage best represents the minimum physiologic channels for MSLT recording?
options: Airflow, effort belts, oximetry, and snore microphone only
EEG from frontal, central, and occipital regions; two EOG channels; chin EMG; and EKG
Leg EMG, capnography, body position, and audio only
One occipital EEG, one EOG, and pulse oximetry only
questions.rationale: The minimum MSLT montage includes EEG from frontal, central, and occipital regions, two EOG channels, chin EMG, and EKG.
questions.source: JCSM 17(12)
questions.coachBob: MSLT asks: did the patient sleep, and did REM appear? The montage must answer both.

questions.question: Which statement correctly distinguishes absolute humidity from relative humidity?
options: Absolute humidity is measured only as a percentage; relative humidity is always measured in mg/L.
Absolute humidity describes airflow resistance; relative humidity describes pressure support.
Absolute humidity is used only in EEG filters; relative humidity is used only in ECG filters.
Absolute humidity is the actual mass of water vapor in gas; relative humidity is the percentage of maximum water vapor capacity at a given temperature.
questions.rationale: Absolute humidity is actual water vapor mass, usually mg/L. Relative humidity is the percent of the gas's maximum capacity at that temperature.
questions.source: Humidification
questions.coachBob: AH is the amount. RH is how full the air is at that temperature.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

Relative humidity is the percentage of the maximum water vapor capacity of the gas at a given temperature.

questions.question: A signal contains frequencies up to 100 Hz. What is the minimum sampling rate needed to satisfy the Nyquist criterion?
options: At least 200 Hz
At least 100 Hz
At least 50 Hz
At least 20 Hz
questions.rationale: The Nyquist criterion requires sampling at least twice the highest signal frequency. Twice 100 Hz is 200 Hz.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Two times the fastest frequency. That is the minimum, not the luxury setting.

questions.question: A 60 Hz signal appears similarly at both amplifier inputs. Which amplifier property helps cancel that in-phase noise?

Common Mode Rejection Ratio
Time in bed
Relative humidity

questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Same noise on both sides should get rejected. That is the beauty of a differential amplifier.

questions.question: A trainee places both EOG electrodes at the same vertical level beside the eyes. What is the most likely problem with this setup?
options: It creates a correct standard EOG montage.
It converts the EOG channel into a leg EMG channel.
It weakens the ability to capture opposite-polarity vertical eye movements used for REM identification.
It eliminates all ECG artifact from the recording.
questions.rationale: Routine EOG placement offsets E1 below the left outer canthus and E2 above the right outer canthus to help capture eye movement polarity patterns.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Placement is not decoration. It is how the signal tells the truth.

questions.question: Which channel set would make an HSAT device inadequate as a Type III respiratory study if used alone?

EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and video only

questions.question: Which finding best identifies a sleep spindle?
options: Rhythmic 11–16 Hz activity, commonly 12–14 Hz, lasting at least 0.5 seconds
A 0.5–2.0 Hz wave greater than 75 µV
A negative sharp wave followed by a positive component lasting at least 0.5 seconds
An 8–13 Hz occipital rhythm during relaxed wakefulness
questions.rationale: Sleep spindles are rhythmic 11–16 Hz bursts, most often 12–14 Hz, with a minimum duration of 0.5 seconds.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Spindles are fast enough to stand apart from slow waves, but not as fast as artifact or muscle.

questions.question: Which pattern best fits NREM parasomnias such as sleepwalking and sleep terrors?
options: They arise from N3 and usually occur in the first third of the night
They arise only from REM in the second half of the night
They require cataplexy and SOREMPs
They are scored only when periodic limb movements are present
questions.rationale: NREM parasomnias include confusional arousals, sleepwalking, and sleep terrors. They typically arise from N3 and cluster in the first third of the night.
questions.source: ICSD-3-TR
questions.coachBob: First third plus N3 points you toward NREM parasomnia.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

They typically arise from N3 sleep and are more common in the first third of the night.

questions.question: Which event pattern best fits REM-related parasomnias or REM phenomena?
options: Sleepwalking and sleep terrors arising from N3 in the first third
REM behavior disorder, sleep paralysis, and nightmares occurring more often in the second half of the night
Periodic limb movements every 5–90 seconds
Central apneas triggered by a PaCO2 drop
questions.rationale: REM-related parasomnias and phenomena include RBD, sleep paralysis, and nightmare disorder. They are linked to REM sleep and often occur later in the night.
questions.source: ICSD-3-TR
questions.coachBob: Second half of the night often means more REM is available.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

They occur during REM sleep and are more common in the second half of the night.

questions.question: For tonic REM sleep without atonia, what is the key pattern being assessed?
options: Only a single leg movement separated by 90 seconds
A thermal airflow drop lasting 10 seconds
Sustained elevation of chin EMG during a large portion of a REM epoch
A K-complex followed by a spindle
questions.rationale: Tonic REM sleep without atonia involves sustained elevation of chin EMG activity during REM, commonly assessed across a large portion of the epoch.
questions.source: Tech Guide
questions.coachBob: RBD evaluation is not just a story from the bed partner. The chin and limb EMG matter.

questions.question: Which description best matches sleep paralysis?
options: Complete amnesia after a sleep terror from N3
A series of 4 limb movements separated by 5–90 seconds
A central apnea caused by hypocapnia only during NREM
Inability to move at sleep onset or sleep offset despite awareness
questions.rationale: Sleep paralysis is the inability to move at sleep onset or sleep offset despite awareness.
questions.source: Chokroverty
questions.coachBob: Sleep paralysis is awareness with REM atonia bleeding into the edge of sleep or wake.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

questions.question: Which description best fits sleep drunkenness?
options: Severe sleep inertia with impaired cognition and disorientation after awakening
Sudden loss of muscle tone triggered by emotion
A 30% flow reduction with 3% desaturation
A benign 11–16 Hz burst in N2
questions.rationale: Sleep drunkenness is a severe form or variant of confusional arousal with prolonged sleep inertia, impaired cognition, and disorientation.
questions.source: Chokroverty
questions.coachBob: Not every hard awakening is ordinary grogginess. Duration and confusion matter.

questions.question: Which adult event best meets AASM hypopnea Rule 1A?
options: At least a 90% thermal drop for 3 seconds without desaturation
A 10% snore increase with no arousal
At least a 30% flow reduction for at least 10 seconds with either at least 3% desaturation or an arousal
Any central pause shorter than 5 seconds
questions.rationale: Rule 1A hypopnea requires at least a 30% flow reduction for at least 10 seconds plus at least 3% desaturation or an associated arousal.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Hypopnea is not just smaller airflow. It needs the duration and the desat or arousal link.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.
questions.bestMatchExcerpt: Domain: 3 | Task_ID: 3D | Source: Fundamentals | Chapter: Ch 39 | Topic: Adult Apnea | Asset_Type: Rule | Asset_Content: Adult apnea: >=90% drop in peak signal for >=10 seconds using an oronasal thermal sensor.

questions.question: Which statement best separates adult apnea from hypopnea scoring?
options: Both require the same 30% flow drop
Hypopnea requires a 90% thermal drop without arousal
Apnea requires 3% desaturation but hypopnea does not
Apnea uses an approximately 90% airflow drop, while hypopnea uses an approximately 30% flow drop with desaturation or arousal
questions.rationale: Apnea and hypopnea differ by degree of signal reduction and associated criteria. Adult apnea uses a much larger drop, while hypopnea requires a smaller drop plus desaturation or arousal.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Do not score by vibe. Know the drop size, duration, and linked consequence.

questions.question: Which pattern best describes Cheyne-Stokes breathing?
options: Isolated obstructive apnea with continued effort
Repeated central events separated by crescendo-decrescendo breathing cycles
Sudden wide-complex tachycardia
Leg movements separated by 5–90 seconds
questions.rationale: Cheyne-Stokes breathing is characterized by cycles of crescendo-decrescendo breathing with central apneas and/or central hypopneas.
questions.source: Fundamentals
questions.coachBob: Look for the waxing and waning pattern, not just one central pause.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.
questions.bestMatchExcerpt: Domain: 3 | Task_ID: 3D | Source: Fundamentals | Chapter: Ch 39 | Topic: Cheyne-Stokes | Asset_Type: Pattern | Asset_Content: Cheyne-Stokes breathing: >=3 consecutive central apneas and/or central hypopneas separated by crescendo-decrescendo breathing with cycle length >=40 seconds, plus either >=5 central apneas/hypopneas per hour associated with the pattern or the pattern lasting >=10 consecutive minutes.

questions.question: Which CO2 pattern meets adult sleep hypoventilation criteria?
options: PCO2 >45 mmHg for 30 seconds only
Any oxygen saturation below 94%
PCO2 >55 mmHg for at least 10 minutes, or a rise of at least 10 mmHg from awake value to >50 mmHg for at least 10 minutes
A PaCO2 drop of 1 mmHg below eupneic baseline
questions.rationale: Adult sleep hypoventilation is scored when PCO2 is >55 mmHg for at least 10 minutes, or when it rises at least 10 mmHg from awake value to >50 mmHg for at least 10 minutes.
questions.source: Fundamentals; AASM V3
questions.coachBob: Oxygen tells you oxygenation. CO2 tells you ventilation. Do not confuse the two.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.
questions.bestMatchExcerpt: Domain: 3 | Task_ID: 3D | Source: Fundamentals | Chapter: Ch 14 | Topic: Adult Hypoventilation | Asset_Type: Rule | Asset_Content: Adult sleep hypoventilation: PCO2 >55 mmHg for >=10 minutes OR an increase in PCO2 >=10 mmHg from awake supine value to >50 mmHg for >=10 minutes.

questions.question: Central chemoreceptors are primarily sensitive to which ventilatory stimulus?
options: Lung stretch alone in the bronchi
Peripheral oxygen saturation only
Airway collapse pressure at the palate
CO2-related pH changes near the ventral medullary surface
questions.rationale: Central chemoreceptors are located near the ventral medullary surface and respond mainly to CO2-related pH changes.
questions.source: ESRS Textbook
questions.coachBob: Central chemoreceptors are CO2 watchdogs.

questions.question: Peripheral chemoreceptors are located primarily in the carotid bodies and respond most strongly to what?
options: Hypoxemia, with additional responses to CO2 and pH
Sleep spindles in central EEG
Upper-airway vibration only
K-complex morphology
questions.rationale: Peripheral chemoreceptors, especially in the carotid bodies, primarily respond to hypoxemia and also respond to CO2 and pH.
questions.source: Pediatric Sleep
questions.coachBob: Carotid bodies are the oxygen sentries.

questions.question: Which breathing pattern is most typical of stable NREM sleep?
options: Highly irregular phasic breathing with skeletal muscle atonia effects
More regular, metabolically controlled breathing with reduced minute ventilation and tidal volume compared with wakefulness
Complete suspension of all chemoreceptor response
Breathing driven entirely by voluntary control
questions.rationale: During NREM sleep, breathing is generally regular and metabolically controlled, though minute ventilation and tidal volume decrease compared with wakefulness.
questions.source: ESRS Textbook
questions.coachBob: NREM breathing is the steadier pattern. REM is the wilder neighborhood.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

questions.question: Which breathing pattern is most typical of REM sleep?
options: Highly regular breathing controlled only by metabolic demand
No change from wakefulness
Irregular breathing with phasic changes in amplitude and frequency
Only obstructive apneas with no central instability
questions.rationale: During REM sleep, breathing is more irregular with phasic changes in amplitude and frequency.

questions.question: During REM sleep, why can patients with diaphragmatic weakness have marked hypoxemia?
options: The diaphragm is completely paralyzed in all normal REM sleep
REM always increases tidal volume above wakefulness
Upper-airway muscles become stronger in REM
Accessory muscles are affected by REM atonia while the diaphragm is relatively spared, so weakness of the diaphragm becomes critical
questions.rationale: The diaphragm is largely spared from REM skeletal muscle atonia. If the diaphragm is weak, loss of accessory muscle support can cause significant REM-related hypoventilation or hypoxemia.
questions.source: ESRS Textbook
questions.coachBob: REM exposes ventilatory weakness because accessory support drops away.
questions.appAction: Live for RPSGTv2.2026 staging by user approval; keep live RPSGT.2026 v8.7.5 untouched unless separately approved.

questions.question: The Starling resistor model describes the upper airway as what?
options: A collapsible tube where flow limitation depends on upstream pressure and downstream mechanics
A rigid pipe that cannot narrow during sleep
A cardiac conduction pathway
A limb EMG filter
questions.rationale: The Starling resistor model conceptualizes the upper airway as a collapsible tube, helping explain flow limitation and airway collapse.
questions.source: Pediatric Sleep
questions.coachBob: The upper airway behaves like a collapsible segment, not steel plumbing.

questions.question: What does a more positive Pcrit indicate?
options: Greater resistance to collapse
Greater upper-airway collapsibility
Lower likelihood of obstructive events
Complete absence of flow limitation
questions.rationale: Pcrit is the intraluminal pressure at which the upper airway collapses. A higher or more positive Pcrit indicates greater collapsibility.
questions.source: Pediatric Sleep
questions.coachBob: More positive Pcrit means the airway collapses more easily.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the Hering-Breuer inflation reflex?
options: A REM-only chin EMG burst rule
A cardiac pause lasting more than 3 seconds
A vagally mediated reflex in which lung inflation helps terminate inspiration
A cortical rhythm that defines N2
questions.rationale: The Hering-Breuer inflation reflex is a vagally mediated reflex where lung inflation contributes to terminating inspiration.
questions.source: Pediatric Sleep
questions.coachBob: Reflexes matter because they shape timing and stability of breathing.

questions.question: In healthy adults, the apneic threshold is approximately how far below eupneic PaCO2?
options: About 20 Torr above eupneic PaCO2
Exactly equal to eupneic PaCO2
Unrelated to carbon dioxide
About 3.5 Torr below eupneic PaCO2
questions.rationale: The apneic threshold is the PaCO2 level below which apnea occurs; in healthy adults it is about 3.5 Torr below eupneic PaCO2.
questions.source: Pediatric Sleep

questions.question: Which statement best describes the menopause-related OSA shift?

questions.question: Which finding best supports an optimal PAP titration result?

RDI <15 without any REM sleep
RDI reduced by 25% with persistent obstructive apneas

questions.question: A report lists N3 minutes as 60 and TST as 300 minutes. What is the N3 percentage?
questions.rationale: Stage percentage equals stage minutes divided by total sleep time, multiplied by 100: 60/300 × 100 = 20%.
questions.source: BRPT blueprint + AASM scoring manual + AAST standard PSG guideline
questions.coachBob: This is plain sleep math. Minutes in the stage over total sleep minutes.
